q: HI: when inventory can't be found AFTER payment is charged, what mechanism undoes prior steps?
why: HI: add compensating actions (like refunds if inventory can't be found) to undo completed steps — the core idea behind the saga pattern.

q: HI: which deep-dive challenges does it raise about running workflows in production? (select all)
options: Handling updates/versioning and migrations to a running workflow
Keeping workflow state size in check
Ensuring a given step runs exactly once
why: HI's deep dives cover workflow versioning/migrations, keeping state size in check, handling external events, and exactly-once steps. Image compression is unrelated.

q: HI references a talk to show how hard reliable multi-step processes are. Which?
options: 'Turning the Database Inside Out' on stream processing
Jimmy Bogard's 'Six Little Lines of Fail' — even a simple sequence of steps is surprisingly hard in distributed systems
why: HI cites Jimmy Bogard's 'Six Little Lines of Fail', whose premise is that distributed systems make even a simple sequence of steps surprisingly hard to do reliably.

q: HI: the typical starting read-to-write ratio for many apps (often climbing higher for content-heavy ones)?
why: HI: the standard ratio starts at 10:1 but often reaches 100:1+ for content-heavy apps like Instagram or YouTube.

q: HI frames the core read-scaling difficulty as ultimately a question of what?
options: Bad software you can debug your way out of
Physics — CPU instructions/sec, memory limits, disk I/O bounds
why: HI: it's usually not a software problem you can debug away; it's physics, where CPU, memory, and disk I/O hit hard constraints more code can't overcome.

q: HI: the natural read-scaling progression?
options: Add caching, then shard, then optimize the database
Optimize within the DB, scale the DB horizontally (replicas/sharding), then add external caching (app + CDN)
Buy a CDN first, then add replicas, then index
Shard first, denormalize, then upgrade hardware
why: HI: optimize within the DB (indexing, hardware, denormalization), then scale horizontally (read replicas, sharding), then add external caching layers.

q: HI: which techniques are in the first bucket — optimizing reads WITHIN your database? (select all)
why: HI lists indexing, hardware upgrades, and denormalization under in-database optimization. CDN/edge caching is a later external layer.

q: HI deep dive: many requests rebuild the SAME expired cache entry at once. What's this called?
options: A thundering-herd / cache stampede where many misses hit the DB at once
A hot partition key on the write path
why: HI: this is the cache stampede / thundering herd — many requests miss simultaneously and hammer the backing database to rebuild the entry.

q: HI: why is the write side often harder than the read side?
options: Bursty, high-throughput writes with lots of contention are a nightmare to build around
Writes are always cached by default
why: HI: read-side tools (replicas, caching) are familiar, but bursty high-throughput writes with contention are hard, and many choices make them worse.

q: HI: which strategies does it combine to scale writes? (select all)
options: Handling bursts with queues and load shedding
why: HI lists exactly four: vertical scaling + DB choices, sharding + partitioning, bursts via queues + load shedding, and batching + hierarchical aggregation.

q: HI: before throwing more hardware at writes, where should you first try to scale?
options: Staying within a single-server, single-database architecture
why: HI: first scale within a single-server, single-database setup (vertical scaling + write optimization) before adding hardware.

q: HI: what does vertical partitioning primarily do (vs horizontal sharding)?
options: Splits rows across many machines by a partition key
Splits a table by columns/fields so different attributes live separately
Replicates the whole table to every node
Caches writes in Redis before persisting
why: HI separates horizontal sharding (splitting rows by key) from vertical partitioning (splitting by columns/attributes).

q: HI deep dive: a hot key too popular for even a single shard. Which two approaches are named? (select all)
why: HI's hot-key deep dive offers Split All Keys and Split Hot Keys Dynamically. Dropping writes or disabling sharding are not the proposed remedies.

q: HI rule of thumb: when should data live in blob storage rather than a database?
options: Over 10MB and it doesn't need SQL queries
why: HI: if something is over 10MB and doesn't need SQL queries, it should probably be in blob storage like S3.

q: HI: the durability figure it cites for object stores like S3?
why: HI: S3 offers 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability, with effectively unlimited capacity and per-object pricing.

q: HI: the core anti-pattern for large-file transfers?
options: Routing file bytes through your application servers as a proxy
why: HI's 'server as a proxy' problem: routing bytes client to server to S3 and back works for small files but breaks down as files grow.

q: HI: which mechanisms avoid proxying bytes through your servers? (select all)
options: Presigned URLs so clients upload directly to blob storage
Resumable uploads with parallel transfers and progress tracking
Storing the full file as a BLOB column in Postgres
why: HI: presigned URLs (direct-to-blob), CDN downloads, and resumable/parallel uploads. Storing the file as a DB BLOB is exactly what it warns against — it kills query, backup, and replication performance.

q: HI deep dive 'What if the upload fails at 99 percent?' — which feature addresses it?
options: Resumable (multipart/chunked) uploads so you don't restart from zero
why: HI pairs the 'fails at 99%' case with resumable uploads: chunked/multipart transfers let an upload resume rather than restart.

q: HI: the two phases the long-running-tasks pattern splits a request into?
why: HI: immediate acknowledgment (validate, enqueue, return a job ID in ms) and background processing by separate workers.

q: HI: why does synchronous processing of a 45-second PDF report fail, beyond poor UX?
options: The database runs out of rows
Most web servers and load balancers enforce ~30-60s timeouts, so the request may not even complete
Browsers refuse all requests over 10 seconds
why: HI: the 45s report often won't complete synchronously because servers/LBs enforce ~30-60s timeout limits, on top of the user staring at a spinner.

q: HI: in the immediate-acknowledgment phase, what does the web server return almost instantly?
options: A job ID after validating and pushing the job to a queue
The raw queue offset and worker IP
why: HI: the web server validates, pushes a job to a queue (Redis/RabbitMQ), and returns a job ID within milliseconds, while workers do the heavy lifting and update status.

q: HI: which are listed as common deep dives in the long-running-tasks article? (select all)
why: HI lists handling failures, repeated failures, preventing duplicate work, queue backpressure, mixed workloads, and job dependencies. CDN region selection belongs to the large blobs pattern.

q: HI: how is the worker side described?
options: A single thread inside the web server
Separate worker processes that continuously poll the queue, grab pending jobs, execute the work, and update job status
The client browser running the task locally
A read replica that auto-executes jobs
why: HI: separate worker processes continuously poll the queue, grab pending jobs, run the time-consuming work, and update job status in a database — decoupled from the web server.

q: HI Bitly: preferred way to generate the short code, and why?
options: Hash the long URL and take the first 7 chars
A unique counter encoded in Base62, so codes are unique by construction
Random 7-char strings with retry on collision
Use the long URL itself, URL-encoded
why: HI leans to counter + Base62: uniqueness is guaranteed, no collision handling needed. Hashing/truncation works but requires a UNIQUE constraint and collision retries; keep that constraint anyway as a safety net.

q: HI Bitly: how do you make the global counter fast and not a per-request bottleneck?
options: A SQL AUTO_INCREMENT column hit on every write
A single Redis atomic INCR, with counter batching (claim ~1000 IDs at a time per server)
A UUID generated on each app server
why: HI: Redis INCR is atomic and fast; batching ID ranges (grab 1000 at once) cuts network round-trips. Losing a few counter values on failover is fine — you only need uniqueness, not a gap-free sequence.

q: HI Bitly: 301 vs 302 redirect — which does HI prefer and why?
options: 301 permanent — browsers cache it, lowering load
302 temporary — keeps requests flowing through your server so you retain control and can add analytics
301 because it is better for SEO
why: HI prefers 302: the browser keeps hitting your server, so you keep control and can later add click analytics. 301 lets the browser cache the redirect (less load, but you lose visibility).

q: HI Dropbox: where should large files be chunked, and via what mechanism?
options: On the server after a single large upload
On the client, using S3 Multipart Upload (per-part presigned URLs)
Inside the database as BLOB rows
why: HI: chunk on the CLIENT (5–10MB), then S3 Multipart Upload — CreateMultipartUpload → presigned URL per part → CompleteMultipartUpload. Server-side chunking defeats the purpose (the big file still flows through your servers).

q: HI Dropbox: what should identify a file/chunk for dedup and resumable uploads?
options: A SHA-256 hash of the content (a fingerprint)
why: HI: fingerprint = SHA-256 of content. It enables dedup (same content already stored) and resumability (which chunks are already present), neither of which a filename can do.

q: HI Dropbox: fixed-size chunks re-upload the whole file when you insert a byte near the start. The fix?
options: Content-Defined Chunking (CDC) with a rolling hash (Rabin fingerprinting)
why: HI: with fixed offsets, an early insert shifts every boundary so all chunks look changed. Content-defined chunking sets boundaries by a rolling hash of the content, so only the edited region's chunks change.

q: HI Ticketmaster: how to hold a seat for ~10 min during checkout without double-booking?
options: A long-running DB transaction holding a row lock
A Redis distributed lock with a TTL that auto-expires if the user abandons
A boolean column flipped with no timeout
why: HI's 'great' answer: Redis lock keyed by seat with a TTL (~10 min) so abandoned holds auto-release. A status+expiration column scanned by cron is the 'good' fallback. The final DB write with ACID is what actually guarantees no double-book.

q: HI Ticketmaster: the staff-level differentiator for a 10M-users-on-one-event on-sale is…
options: A virtual waiting queue that admits users to the booking flow in a controlled trickle
why: HI: a virtual waiting queue protects the booking path from a thundering herd by admitting users in controlled batches — the 'think outside the box' move that signals senior/staff.

q: HI Ticketmaster: why not implement event search with SQL LIKE '%term%'?
options: It forces a full table scan (no index help) — use Elasticsearch for full-text search
why: HI: leading-wildcard LIKE can't use a B-tree index, so it scans the whole table — too slow at scale. Elasticsearch's inverted index is the right tool; add query-result/edge caching.

q: HI News Feed: the standard answer for mixing normal users and celebrities is…
options: Hybrid: precompute feeds for normal users, fan-out-on-read for celebrities, merge at read time
Store no feeds, recompute every request
why: HI's 'great' solution: precompute (push) into a per-user feed table for normal accounts, but pull celebrity posts at read time and merge — avoiding the millions-of-writes-per-celebrity-post explosion.

q: HI News Feed: a few hot posts get hammered and your DynamoDB feed cache develops a hot key. Fix?
options: Replicate the hot entry across multiple cache nodes (redundant post cache) to spread reads
Switch to a single bigger cache node
why: HI: DynamoDB (and single cache nodes) hate hot keys. Replicating the hot post across several cache nodes spreads the read load instead of concentrating it on one partition.

q: HI WhatsApp: sender and receiver are connected to different chat servers. How do you route the message?
options: Redis Pub/Sub with a topic per user, so the receiver's server is subscribed and gets the message
A shared database both servers poll
Consistent hashing so both users always land on one server
why: HI's 'great' answer: Redis Pub/Sub, one topic per user — sidesteps consistent-hashing complexity. A Kafka topic-per-user is an anti-pattern (Kafka isn't built for hundreds of millions of topics).

q: HI WhatsApp: Pub/Sub is at-most-once and stores nothing. How is delivery still guaranteed?
options: Write to a durable Inbox table before publishing, require client acks, and use per-chat sequence numbers with gap detection
Rely on the load balancer to retry
why: HI: persist undelivered messages in an Inbox (delete on ack), and have clients detect gaps via per-chat sequence numbers (piggybacked on heartbeats) with a polling backstop — covering Pub/Sub's dropped messages.

q: HI WhatsApp: how should message ordering be handled?
options: A global total order across all chats
Strict per-chat ordering enforced with locks
NTP-synced server-receipt timestamps; the client sorts by them (accept occasional pop-in)
why: HI: don't pay for strict ordering. Stamp messages with NTP-synced server receipt time and let clients sort; users tolerate rare reordering/pop-in. Client clocks can't be trusted.

q: HI WhatsApp: how do you detect a silently-dead WebSocket connection?
options: Wait for the next user message
Application-level heartbeats (TCP keepalive is too slow)
why: HI: TCP keepalive intervals are too coarse; use application-level heartbeats to spot dead connections quickly (and derive 'last seen' from active connections rather than DB writes per heartbeat).

q: HI Uber: 10M drivers pinging location every few seconds (~2M writes/sec). Where do you store it?
options: An in-memory geospatial store like Redis (geohashing / quadtree)
A Postgres table with a B-tree on (lat, long)
why: HI: that write rate in DynamoDB would cost a fortune (>$200k/day), and a B-tree can't do 2D proximity. Redis with geohashing handles high-frequency writes and radius queries in memory; add adaptive update intervals client-side.

q: HI Uber: a driver doesn't respond to a ride offer. What's the robust way to handle the multi-step timeout/retry flow?
options: Durable execution (e.g. Temporal/Cadence) to drive the offer→timeout→reoffer workflow reliably
why: HI: this is the multi-step-process pattern. Durable execution (Uber authored Cadence) survives crashes and reliably advances offer → 10s timeout → next driver, which a fragile delay queue can't guarantee.

q: HI Uber: which values must NOT be trusted from the client?
options: userId, timestamps, and the fare estimate — derive them from the session/server/DB
why: HI repeatedly warns: never trust client-supplied identity or money fields. Pull userId from the session/JWT, set timestamps server-side, and re-read the fare from the DB by fareId.

q: HI Ad Click Aggregator: the recommended real-time processing pipeline is…
options: Write clicks to Postgres and run GROUP BY on query
Stream (Kafka/Kinesis) → Flink → an OLAP store (e.g. ClickHouse/Druid/BigQuery)
why: HI: ingest into a stream, aggregate in Flink, write to an OLAP DB optimized for analytical queries. Using one transactional DB for both ingest and analytics is the anti-pattern.

q: HI Ad Click Aggregator: a viral ad creates a hot partition. The standard fix?
options: Append a random suffix to the partition key (AdId:0..N); the aggregator strips it and SUMs the parts
why: HI: salt the key with a random suffix so the hot ad's events spread across partitions; the aggregator removes the suffix and sums per AdId. Classic hot-shard remediation.

q: HI Ad Click Aggregator: how do you guarantee correctness against the fast-but-lossy stream path?
options: Trust the stream; it never loses data
Lambda architecture: also dump raw events to S3 and run a nightly Spark batch as the source of truth, reconciling against the stream
why: HI: speed layer (Flink) for near-real-time + batch layer (Spark over an S3 data lake) as the authoritative recompute. Reconciling the two catches drift/loss in the streaming path.
